understanding.goal_understanding: The overall goal of the game was to complete each level by achieving a specific win condition, typically getting at least one pushable object onto a goal tile marked with '+'. Once a level’s win condition was satisfied, the engine reported WIN and the whole game run was considered successful. Each level had a limited step budget, so the goal also implicitly included planning efficient actions within that budget.
understanding.mechanics_understanding:
Movement: You control a currently selected piece. MOVE_UP, MOVE_DOWN, MOVE_LEFT, and MOVE_RIGHT attempt to move that selected piece one tile in the chosen direction. If the move is invalid (because of a wall, the edge of the board, or some other blocking element), the action still consumes a step but the piece’s position does not change.

Pushing: Some objects are pushable. When your selected piece moves into a tile occupied by a pushable object and the tile beyond that object is empty and not blocked, the move can push that object one tile further in the same direction. If the tile beyond is blocked or off-board, the push (and therefore the move) is prevented. The usual Sokoban-style logic seems to apply: you can push, but not pull, and you cannot push multiple solid obstacles if the path is not clear.

Walls/Obstacles: Certain grid tiles act as hard walls or obstacles. Attempting to MOVE into a wall (or out of bounds) leaves the selected piece in place with a message that it is blocked or that the action had no movement effect. These obstacles also stop pushable objects; they cannot be pushed through walls.

Selection and CLICK: CLICK actions are used to select or switch between pieces. When you CLICK on a coordinate that contains a selectable piece, control jumps to that piece and it becomes the active one for subsequent MOVE actions. In later turns, CLICK [x, y] was explicitly reported as moving the selected piece from its previous coordinates to the clicked coordinates, which indicates that, at least in that context, CLICK also functioned as a teleport-style selection/move: it directly relocated the selected piece to the target tile instead of merely changing selection. This suggests two related mechanics: (1) you can change which entity is selected by clicking on it, and (2) in some situations or levels, CLICK [x, y] may reposition the selected piece to those coordinates (subject to whatever hidden constraints applied in that level).

Step budget: Every action, whether a MOVE or CLICK, consumes one step. When steps reach 0, the run ends immediately, regardless of whether the level is solved.

Overall: To win, you must use CLICK to get control of or reposition the right piece(s), then use directional MOVE actions (and pushing when possible) to place a pushable object onto a '+' goal tile before the step budget runs out.

history.summary: Level 1 step budget exhausted.
Agent explained goal and mechanics.

understanding.goal_understanding: The overall goal of the game was to complete each level by achieving a specific win condition, typically getting at least one pushable object onto a goal tile marked with '+'. Once a level’s win condition was satisfied, the engine reported WIN and the whole game run was considered successful. Each level had a limited step budget, so the goal also implicitly included planning efficient actions within that budget.
understanding.mechanics_understanding:
Movement: You control a currently selected piece. MOVE_UP, MOVE_DOWN, MOVE_LEFT, and MOVE_RIGHT attempt to move that selected piece one tile in the chosen direction. If the move is invalid (because of a wall, the edge of the board, or some other blocking element), the action still consumes a step but the piece’s position does not change.

Pushing: Some objects are pushable. When your selected piece moves into a tile occupied by a pushable object and the tile beyond that object is empty and not blocked, the move can push that object one tile further in the same direction. If the tile beyond is blocked or off-board, the push (and therefore the move) is prevented. The usual Sokoban-style logic seems to apply: you can push, but not pull, and you cannot push multiple solid obstacles if the path is not clear.

Walls/Obstacles: Certain grid tiles act as hard walls or obstacles. Attempting to MOVE into a wall (or out of bounds) leaves the selected piece in place with a message that it is blocked or that the action had no movement effect. These obstacles also stop pushable objects; they cannot be pushed through walls.

Selection and CLICK: CLICK actions are used to select or switch between pieces. When you CLICK on a coordinate that contains a selectable piece, control jumps to that piece and it becomes the active one for subsequent MOVE actions. In later turns, CLICK [x, y] was explicitly reported as moving the selected piece from its previous coordinates to the clicked coordinates, which indicates that, at least in that context, CLICK also functioned as a teleport-style selection/move: it directly relocated the selected piece to the target tile instead of merely changing selection. This suggests two related mechanics: (1) you can change which entity is selected by clicking on it, and (2) in some situations or levels, CLICK [x, y] may reposition the selected piece to those coordinates (subject to whatever hidden constraints applied in that level).

Step budget: Every action, whether a MOVE or CLICK, consumes one step. When steps reach 0, the run ends immediately, regardless of whether the level is solved.

Overall: To win, you must use CLICK to get control of or reposition the right piece(s), then use directional MOVE actions (and pushing when possible) to place a pushable object onto a '+' goal tile before the step budget runs out.
